entry.title: Activating Document Grounding with Joule in SAP BTP
entry.content:
Dear community,

I have a question about activating Document Grounding in SAP BTP: Do AI Core units, Joule, and Document Grounding need to be in the same global account or the same subaccount?

In the prerequisites, the documentation says: “You’ve added the entitlements for Document Grounding to the subaccount where you’ve subscribed to Joule,” and at the same time: “You’ve purchased the AI Unit SKU (8019164) and AI units. After you’ve done so, the entitlements for Document Grounding are automatically added to your global account.”

Does this mean that all three - AI units, Joule, and the Document Grounding instance - must be in the same global account and subaccount? Or are there any exceptions?

entry.title: User Filtering for Repository Synchronization in SAP Identity Access Governance
entry.content:
Introduction:

In the Standard Edition of SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance (IAG), subscription consumption is calculated using the Resources metric. This metric represents the number of unique users, including technical users, synchronized from on‑premise and/or cloud systems into SAP IAG.

More information available on: SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance - License Metric 

3262845 - How to monitor IAG usage? 

In many customers’ landscapes, not all users maintained in target systems require identity governance. Test users, technical users, and generic accounts typically do not participate in provisioning, access risk analysis, or access certification. Synchronizing such users into SAP IAG may unnecessarily increase subscription consumption without delivering business value.

To address this, SAP IAG provides user filtering capabilities during repository synchronization, enabling customers to limit synchronization to governance‑relevant users only. This blog outlines the available filtering options across common SAP IAG integration scenarios.

Integration Scenarios in SAP IAG Standard Edition

Depending on the target application, SAP IAG supports the following integration models:

  1. On‑premise SAP ABAP systems
    Connected via SAP Cloud Connector
    Examples: SAP ECC, SAP S/4HANA (On‑Premise)
  2. SAP cloud applications integrated using SAP Identity Provisioning Service (IPS)
    Connected through a proxy system
    Examples: SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS), SAP Ariba, SAP Analytics Cloud
  3. SAP cloud applications integrated directly using SAP BTP destinations
    Examples: SAP Concur, SAP IBP, SAP Marketing Cloud, SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

Each integration model offers distinct options for filtering users during repository synchronization.

1. User Filtering for Applications Integrated via SAP IPS Proxy

For applications integrated through SAP Identity Provisioning Service (IPS), user filtering is implemented using transformation rules in the proxy system. These rules determine which users are synchronized into SAP IAG during repository sync.

Example: Filtering Users from SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS)

In this scenario, users are filtered based on IAS user group membership:

  • A group named Z_SAP_IAG_TEST is maintained in SAP IAS.
  • An IPS transformation rule is configured to synchronize only users assigned to this group.
  • During repository synchronization, only users belonging to this group are synced into SAP IAG, while all other IAS users are excluded.

This approach ensures that only relevant users are onboarded into SAP IAG.

Currently there are 16 users assigned to this group in SAP IAS.

On execution of repository object sync in SAP IAG for SAP IAS, only 16 users got synchronized.

Sample Transformation Filter Expressions

Filters can be defined using various user attributes, depending on the application. Below are few examples:

SAP IAS

ias.user.filter = userName eq "john.doe"
ias.user.filter = userName co "admin"
ias.user.filter = groups.display eq "Finance_Team" or groups.display eq "HR_Team"

SAP Analytics Cloud

sac.user.filter = name.familyName eq "Smith" and addresses.country eq "Germany"
sac.user.filter = userName sw "SAP_"

SAP Ariba

ariba.applications.user.filter = userName eq "SmithJ"
ariba.applications.user.filter = name.familyName eq "Johnson" and addresses.country eq "USA"
ariba.applications.user.filter = userName sw "PROC_"

These filters allow precise control over which users are synchronized based on naming patterns, attributes, or group assignments.

2. User Filtering for Applications Integrated via BTP Destination or Cloud Connector

Some SAP cloud applications do not require an IPS proxy and can be integrated directly using SAP BTP destinations.

Similarly, on‑premise ABAP systems first establish connectivity through SAP Cloud Connector, followed by destination configuration in the BTP subaccount.

After integration, each target system is represented as an Application in SAP IAG under Administration.

Using the User Filter Option in SAP IAG

SAP IAG provides a User Filter option at the application level:

  • Filters are defined using attributes such as User ID, User Group, or User Type (availability depends on the application).
  • Currently, SAP IAG supports exclude‑only filtering.
  • Users matching the exclusion criteria are not synchronized into SAP IAG during repository sync.

 

 Example: Excluding Users from SAP Concur

In this example:

  • Specific Concur user IDs are configured in the exclusion filter.
  • Multiple exclusion conditions can be added.
  • During repository synchronization, excluded users are no longer synchronized into SAP IAG.

This approach is effective for excluding technical or generic users across recurring synchronizations.

On‑Premise ABAP Systems (ECC / S/4HANA)

For SAP ECC and SAP S/4HANA (On‑Premise):

  • Exclusion filters can be applied using User ID, User Group and User Type.
  • For advanced requirements, SAP provides an enhancement option via enhancement spot in SIAG_USER_GET_LIST function module and BADI implementation.

Relevant SAP Note:2802767 - Enhancement for filtering users in SAP ECC and S/4 HANA OP sync to IAG 

3. User Filtering for SAP SuccessFactors

SAP SuccessFactors offers a dedicated filtering mechanism using application parameters in SAP IAG.

Configuration Details

  • Navigate to Administration -> Configuration application in SAP IAG
    • Configuration Group: SUCCESSFACTORS_SYNC
    • Parameter Name: USER_FILTER
    • Parameter Value: condition for different attributes and values.

Filtering is based on attributes exposed by the SuccessFactors OData API.

Examples of Filter Conditions

1. Synchronize only active employees:

Parameter value: status eq 't'

2. Synchronize only permanent employees:

empInfo/JobInfoNav/employmentType eq '3637'

If the attribute is not the top-level service, OData navigation paths must be used. Here the navigation is empInfo-> JobInfoNav- > employmentType

 Multiple conditions can be combined using the ‘and’ operator.

Refer SAP document for more information: Application Parameters | SAP help portal

 

Conclusion

User filtering during repository synchronization is a key capability in SAP Identity Access Governance that helps customers:

  • Optimize subscription consumption
  • Avoid onboarding non-governance‑relevant users
  • Maintain a lean and efficient identity landscape

By using IPS transformation rules, application‑level exclusion filters, enhancement options for on‑premise systems, and SuccessFactors application parameters, customers can align SAP IAG synchronization precisely with their governance requirements.

entry.title: Understanding Log Levels in SAP Cloud Integration (CPI)
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) provides different log levels to monitor and troubleshoot iFlows effectively. Many beginners find it confusing to understand when to use Info, Error, Debug, and Trace and how each level affects message visibility.

In this blog, I will explain each log level in a simple way along with practical usage.

What Are Log Levels in SAP CPI?

Log levels define how much information CPI captures while processing a message.

Choosing the correct log level helps you:

  • Monitor message execution
  • Troubleshoot issues efficiently
  • Avoid unnecessary performance overhead

Log Configuration in CPI

Below is the Log Configuration screen where you select the log level before running an iFlow:

 

 

 

 

 

Explanation of Each Log Level:

None         

  • No logs are recorded
  • Used for high-volume or performance-critical scenarios
  • Not recommended for troubleshooting 

Info (Default)

Captures basic events:

  • Message start
  •  Message completion
  • High-level status

Suitable for regular monitoring

Recommended for production

Error

  • Logs only when an error occurs

  • Helps identify failures without excessive logging

  • Useful in production environments

Debug    

  • Provides step-by-step execution details

  • Does NOT capture full payload

  • Helps understand flow behavior

Trace    

Captures complete details:

Payload before and after each step

  • Mapping output
  • Router conditions
  • Splitter behavior

Best for deep troubleshooting

Should be used temporarily (can impact performance)

How CPI Internally Behaves Based on Log Levels

When an iFlow runs, CPI behaves differently depending on the selected log level:

  • For Info / Error → Only summary logs are stored
  • For Debug → Step-level information is captured
  • For Trace → Full payload snapshots are stored at every step

This directly affects:

  • Message Processing Logs (MPL)
  • Trace log visibility
  • System performance

Conclusion

Log Levels are a fundamental part of SAP CPI monitoring and debugging.

Understanding when to use each level helps you:

  • Diagnose issues faster
  • Improve performance
  • Avoid unnecessary logging

Note: This blog is based on my personal learning and hands-on experience with SAP Cloud Integration (CPI).

#SAPCPI #SAPIntegration #BTP #Logging

@Bibekananda1231 

@Souravraj_Maitra

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: CPFH Sales Kits in Consignment
entry.content:
Introduction

With release 2402, support for Sales Kits of type CPFH in consignment processing was delivered.

At first sight, this may look like a limited scope enhancement: allowing a Sales Kit to be used in consignment scenarios. In reality, the change is much more significant. The real requirement was not just order entry support, but a process model that preserves the commercial meaning of the kit while still allowing the system to execute the logistics and inventory steps on component level.

This is exactly where CPFH becomes relevant.

In a CPFH Sales Kit, the processing logic is clearly split:

  • the main item carries the commercial meaning and pricing
  • the subitems carry the delivery- and inventory-relevant execution

This split is particularly important in consignment processing, because consignment is not just a sales-document-driven scenario. It is a stock-oriented business process with distinct follow-up steps:

  • Consignment Fill-Up
  • Consignment Issue
  • Consignment Pick-Up
  • Consignment Return

For a normal material, these steps are straightforward. For a Sales Kit, they are not. The customer orders one commercial object, but the system needs to execute on multiple physical components.

This blog explains how CPFH Sales Kits are processed in consignment, what the business meaning of the relevant item categories is, and how the solution integrates into the standard consignment execution framework.

Business Background

Among the different Sales Kit types, CPFH is especially suitable for consignment because it already reflects the separation that consignment processing needs:

  • commercial meaning on header level
  • execution meaning on component level

From a business perspective, this allows the system to preserve the kit as the object the customer sees and buys.

From an execution perspective, it allows the system to process the components that are physically delivered, consumed, picked up, or returned.

Without such a split, a kit might still look correct in the sales document, but the downstream process would become inconsistent. The document would show one object, while the actual stock movements would need another.

The core design principle is therefore simple:

The header keeps the kit semantics. The subitems carry the consignment execution semantics.

Scope Delivered in 2402

The current delivery scope focuses on Sales Kits of type CPFH for the following consignment process steps:

  1. Consignment Fill-Up
  2. Consignment Issue
  3. Consignment Pick-Up
  4. Consignment Return

Across all four steps, the solution distinguishes between:

  • header item categories, which define the business role of the kit header
  • subitem item categories, which define the execution role of the components

This distinction is essential. The different item categories are not technical duplicates. They exist because the business meaning of the kit header is not the same in Fill-Up, Issue, Pick-Up, and Return.

Overall Processing Logic

The following table summarizes the end-to-end logic of CPFH in consignment.

Table 1: CPFH in Consignment – Overall Logic

Business Step Header Item Category Header Meaning Subitem Item Category Subitem Meaning Schedule Line Movement Type
Fill-UpCF3HKit header, preserves kit semantics, not used as a normal revenue-relevant billing itemCF3IComponents moved into customer consignment stockE1 / E0631
IssueCPHDPriced and billing-relevant kit headerCI3LComponents actually consumed / issuedC1 / C0633
IssueCFHDNot priced on header, but still billing-relevantCI3LComponents actually consumed / issuedC1 / C0633
IssueCNHDNon-priced, non-billing header, used only to preserve kit structureCI3FComponents actually consumed / issuedC1 / C0633
Pick-UpCP3HKit header for pick-up processingCP3IComponents picked up from customer consignment stockF1 / F0632
ReturnCR3PPriced and billing-relevant return headerCR3LComponents processed in returnD0634
ReturnCR3FNot priced on header, but still billing-relevantCR3LComponents processed in returnD0634
ReturnCR3NNon-priced, non-billing header, used only to preserve kit structureCR3IComponents processed in returnD0634

This table highlights the key principle of the solution:

  • the header categories differentiate the business role of the kit header
  • the subitem categories carry the actual execution logic
  • the stock-relevant processing always happens on subitem level

Consignment Fill-Up

Business Meaning

In Consignment Fill-Up, stock is moved to customer consignment stock.
At this stage, the customer has not yet actually consumed the goods. Therefore, Fill-Up is not a revenue event. It is much closer to a stock transfer into customer consignment stock.

That business meaning is reflected in the item category design.

Header: CF3H

The CPFH header item category in Fill-Up is CF3H.

Its purpose is to:

  • preserve the Sales Kit header in the document
  • keep the commercial representation of the kit
  • provide the structural basis for the exploded subitems

At the same time, CF3H is not the execution-relevant stock item. And because Fill-Up is not the point of revenue realization, it is not intended for normal billing logic. In practice, it fits pro forma invoice semantics rather than standard billing.

So the role of CF3H is not to execute the stock movement, but to keep the kit visible and meaningful in the process.

Subitem: CF3I

The actual execution in Fill-Up happens on CF3I.

CF3I represents:

  • the exploded component line
  • the material actually moved into customer consignment stock
  • the execution-relevant line for Fill-Up

It is mapped to:

  • E1 / E0
  • movement type 631

So the Fill-Up logic can be summarized as:

CF3H keeps the kit header. CF3I performs the actual fill-up execution.

Consignment Issue

Issue is the most commercially relevant step in consignment, because this is the point where the customer actually consumes stock from customer consignment inventory.

This is why the system distinguishes between multiple header item categories for Issue. They are not redundant. They reflect different business meanings of the kit header.

CPHD – Priced and Billing-Relevant Issue Header

CPHD represents the most direct commercial Issue path.

Its meaning is:

  • the header is priced
  • the header is billing-relevant
  • the kit has entered a commercial settlement path

The corresponding execution-relevant subitem is CI3L.

So CPHD + CI3L represents:

A commercially relevant kit header combined with component-level issue execution.

CFHD – Not Priced on Header, but Still Billing-Relevant

CFHD differs from CPHD.

Here, the header does not behave like a standard priced header item, but it still remains billing-relevant. This makes CFHD an alternative Issue path where commercial relevance is preserved, but header pricing is not the defining characteristic.

The corresponding subitem is also CI3L.

CNHD – Non-Priced and Non-Billing Issue Header

CNHD is especially important to understand.

Its role is:

  • no pricing
  • no billing
  • the header exists only to preserve the structural semantics of the kit

This means CNHD is not a commercial settlement header. It is a structural header. The document still needs a valid kit header, even though the actual execution happens on the components.

The corresponding subitem is CI3F.

So CNHD + CI3F represents:

A purely structural Issue header with actual component-level execution.

Subitems in Issue: CI3L and CI3F

The actual Issue execution is always done on the subitems, not on the header.

The relevant subitem categories are:

  • CI3L
  • CI3F

Both are mapped to:

  • C1 / C0
  • movement type 633

Their common role is clear:

They carry the actual component-level consumption of consignment stock.

Consignment Pick-Up

Consignment Pick-Up retrieves stock from customer consignment stock that has not yet been consumed.

Again, the business document keeps the kit header, but execution happens on the components.

Header: CP3H

CP3H is the CPFH header item category for Pick-Up.

Its purpose is to:

  • preserve the kit structure in the document
  • keep the kit header visible in pick-up processing
  • provide the structural basis for the component lines

Subitem: CP3I

The actual Pick-Up execution happens on CP3I.

It represents:

  • the components being retrieved
  • the stock-relevant line in pick-up processing

It is mapped to:

  • F1 / F0
  • movement type 632

So Pick-Up follows the same overall principle:

CP3H preserves the kit meaning, while CP3I performs the actual component-level retrieval.

Consignment Return

Return processing again differentiates between multiple header paths, because not every return header has the same commercial meaning.

CR3P – Priced and Billing-Relevant Return Header

CR3P represents:

  • a priced header
  • a billing-relevant return path
  • a commercially relevant return header for the kit

The corresponding subitem is CR3L.

So CR3P + CR3L means:

A commercially relevant return header with actual component-level return execution.

CR3F – Not Priced on Header, but Still Billing-Relevant

CR3F is conceptually similar to CFHD in Issue.

Its meaning is:

  • the header is not priced in the normal way
  • but it remains billing-relevant

The corresponding subitem is also CR3L.

CR3N – Non-Priced and Non-Billing Return Header

CR3N is the Return counterpart of CNHD.

Its role is:

  • no pricing
  • no billing
  • the header exists only to preserve the structural semantics of the kit

The corresponding execution-relevant subitem is CR3I.

So CR3N + CR3I means:

A purely structural return header with actual component-level return execution.

Subitems in Return: CR3L and CR3I

The actual Return execution happens on:

  • CR3L
  • CR3I

These are mapped to:

  • D0
  • movement type 634

So even in Return, the business pattern remains unchanged:

The header keeps the kit meaning, while the subitem performs the actual return execution.

Relationship to Standard Consignment Item Categories

A helpful way to understand the design is to compare the CPFH subitems with the standard consignment item categories.

Table 2: Standard vs. CPFH Execution Paths

Standard Item Category Business Step CPFH Subitem Item Category Meaning
CFNFill-UpCF3IStandard fill-up item vs. CPFH component fill-up item
CIN / CCIN / CCXNIssueCI3L / CI3FStandard issue item vs. CPFH component issue item
CPNPick-UpCP3IStandard pick-up item vs. CPFH component pick-up item
CRN / CRBN / CCRNReturnCR3L / CR3IStandard return item vs. CPFH component return item

This comparison makes one design principle very clear:

The solution does not create a new consignment execution model. It adds a header/subitem split on top of the standard consignment execution patterns.

In other words:

  • for a normal material, the system directly uses the standard item category path
  • for a CPFH kit, the system keeps the kit header and lets the subitems reuse the standard execution behavior

Technical Design Perspective

From a technical point of view, the implementation can be understood in three layers.

1. Item Category Determination

The system first determines the header item category, and then derives the subitem item category based on the higher-level item category.

This allows the document to preserve the kit structure while still generating execution-relevant component lines.

2. Business Role of Header and Subitem

The header item categories distinguish whether the kit header is:

  • priced
  • billing-relevant
  • or purely structural

The subitem item categories distinguish the actual execution role of the components for:

  • Fill-Up
  • Issue
  • Pick-Up
  • Return

3. Schedule Line Determination and Movement Execution

The subitems are then mapped back to the standard consignment execution framework:

  • Fill-Up: E1 / E0 → 631
  • Issue: C1 / C0 → 633
  • Pick-Up: F1 / F0 → 632
  • Return: D0 → 634

So the real innovation of the solution is not a new movement model. The innovation is:

making the CPFH kit structure executable within the standard consignment framework.

Roadmap Outlook

With the delivery of CPFH support in consignment processing in 2402, the first step has been taken to enable Sales Kits in this area.

Support for additional Sales Kit types, including ERLA, DISH, and LUMF, is planned to be introduced gradually in future releases after 2702, further expanding the overall coverage of Sales Kits in consignment scenarios.

Conclusion

Supporting CPFH in consignment is not just about allowing a Sales Kit in a consignment order. It addresses a more fundamental business and technical requirement:

How can a Sales Kit remain a kit from a commercial perspective, while still being executed consistently on component level in consignment processing?

The answer is based on three principles:

  1. The header keeps the kit semantics
    Different header item categories distinguish whether the header is priced, billing-relevant, or purely structural.
  2. The subitems carry the execution semantics
    The real inventory and movement-relevant processing happens on the component-level subitems.
  3. The execution layer reuses the standard consignment framework
    Standard schedule line categories and movement types are reused, so CPFH can run consistently within existing consignment processing.

With the 2402 delivery, CPFH Sales Kits can now be processed in consignment while preserving both:

  • the commercial representation of the kit
  • the execution logic of the components

This also lays the groundwork for further support of additional Sales Kit types in consignment scenarios in future releases.

entry.title: Fresh Look & Translatable: Our Click-Through Tutorials Got a Makeover
entry.content:
Our click-through tutorials have long been a helpful companion for navigating SAP Central Business Configuration. Now, they’ve just become even better. We’re excited to share a set of improvements designed to make your learning experience more modern and intuitive.

Design Upgrade

The click-through tutorials have received a major design overhaul. The new, refreshed look feels more modern and state of the art, making it easier and more enjoyable to follow each step. The upgraded design aligns with the latest SAP design language, ensuring a consistent user experience across SAP solutions. Whether you're exploring a process for the first time or using the tutorials as a quick refresher, the cleaner layout helps keep the focus exactly where you need it.

The new tutorial start page

Machine Translation

Did you know there’s a built-in translation feature? If English isn’t your preferred language, you can automatically translate all tutorial texts with a single click. Just select the translate icon in the top-right corner and choose from a wide range of languages – German, French, Spanish, Japanese, and many more.

Please note that all translated texts are machine generated. As such, SAP cannot provide any warranty regarding their accuracy or completeness. We appreciate your understanding and hope this feature makes the tutorials more accessible for users around the world.

Machine translation feature

Tutorial Access

There are two easy ways to access the click-through tutorials. In SAP Central Business Configuration, choose the question mark icon in the upper-right corner to open the in-app help and navigate to Learning. Alternatively, you can access the tutorials via this SAP Help Portal page.

Learning section in the in-app help

Did you like this blog? If so, please leave us a thumbs up at the top. Also, make sure to follow the SAP Central Business Configuration tag or follow this dedicated blog collection to stay up to date with the latest blog posts.

Do you have any ideas on how to further enhance SAP Central Business Configuration? Go to the Customer Influence platform to submit an improvement request or vote for the ideas of others. This helps us understand your requirements even better and prioritize our work accordingly. We’re looking forward to your improvement requests.

entry.title: Dynamic Watermark in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Output Management: A Step-by-Step Guide
entry.content:
Recently, I got one of those requirements that sounds incredibly simple on paper but turns into an interesting technical puzzle once you work on S4HPC.

The business wanted a conditional watermark on their Purchase Order Output Form. If the PO is still navigating the approval workflow, it needs to display a massive "DRAFT" watermark across all pages. Once fully approved and released, that watermark needs to disappear completely.

The idea is straightforward: add custom logic to make the watermark conditional at both, form template level and the data source level. But as anyone who works with SAP Output Management knows, getting the Master Form and Content Form to talk to each other isn't always plug-and-play.

Here is how I did it.

Step 1: The Data Source Dilemma

Since the watermark needs to appear on all pages, the logical place to put it is the master form template.

First, I checked the standard PO form data source FDP_EF_PURCHASE_ORDER_SRV to see if I could simply grab the PurchasingProcessingStatus and ReleaseIsNotCompleted fields of I_PurchaseOrderAPI01 view to build my condition. Unsurprisingly, they weren't there.

My next thought was to extend the standard data source via the Custom Fields app. However, FDP_EF_PURCHASE_ORDER_SRV cannot be extended.

After some digging, I came across SAP Note 3525721, which states that custom fields can be used for the PO output data source with the use BAdI MMPUR_PO_OUTPUT_HEADER_EXT.

 

Step 2: Adding the Custom Field via BAdI

First, I went to the Custom Fields app (Business Context: Procurement: Purchasing Document) and created a new Text(1) or Indicator field called yy1_po_wf_approved_pdh. I made sure to enable its usage for the respective Form Templates.

Next, I implemented the BAdI to determine the workflow status. Here is the ABAP logic:

SELECT SINGLE FROM i_purchaseorderapi01 WITH PRIVILEGED ACCESS
  FIELDS 
  WHERE purchaseorder = @purchaseorder
    AND releaseisnotcompleted = _false
    AND purchasingprocessingstatus = '05'
  INTO (po_approved).
IF sy-subrc = 0.
  purdoc_extension-yy1_po_wf_approved_pdh = 'X'.
ENDIF.

 After downloading the Content Form XML/XDP again, the newly added custom field was sitting right there in the Data View palette.

Step 3: Form Layout Setup

Now, let’s jump into Adobe LiveCycle Designer to actually build the layout.

I added the DRAFT image inside a subform (named formWatermark) on both of the Master Pages: mst1 (the first page) and mst2 (subsequent pages).

Note: Make sure your formWatermark subform is set to Positioned (not Flowed) in the Object palette, and right-click to select Send to Back. This ensures it acts as a true background watermark and doesn't push your main content areas down.

Here is where we hit the major roadblock: How do we access the master page watermark subform from the content form? Or conversely, how do we access the content form data from the master page?

This can be achieved from either side using a simple xfa.resolveNode trick. You only need to use one of the following approaches.

Note: For both approaches, ensure your Script Editor dropdowns are set to Language: JavaScript and Run At: Server. In S/4HANA Public Cloud, form rendering happens on the Server. 

Approach #1: Controlling it from the Master Template Form

To access our custom field value from the Content template form dataset within the Master template form, we need the absolute path of the field from the Content template's data view (e.g., PurchaseOrderNode.YY1_PO_WF_APPROVED_PDH).

We concatenate xfa.datasets.data.Form. with our custom field path. Place this JavaScript logic on the formWatermark subform's initialize event:

var WFStatusNode = xfa.resolveNode("xfa.datasets.data.Form.PurchaseOrderNode.YY1_PO_WF_APPROVED_PDH");

if (WFStatusNode.value === "X") {
    this.presence = "hidden";
} else {
    this.presence = "visible";
}

Approach #2: Controlling it from the Content Template Form

Alternatively, we can access the master page watermark subforms directly from within the content template form by prefixing the path with #pageSet[0]..

Place this JavaScript code on the YY1_PO_WF_APPROVED_PDH field's initialize event:

var firstPageWatermarkNode = xfa.resolveNode("#pageSet[0].mst1.formWatermark");
var subsequentPageWatermarkNode = xfa.resolveNode("#pageSet[0].mst2.formWatermark");

if (this.rawValue === "X") {
    firstPageWatermarkNode.presence = "hidden";
    subsequentPageWatermarkNode.presence = "hidden";
} else {
    firstPageWatermarkNode.presence = "visible";
    subsequentPageWatermarkNode.presence = "visible";
}

The Final Result

After choosing your preferred approach, upload the forms (Master and Content) back into the system and test your output.

When the PO is still in approval:

And once the approval is fully completed:

The draft watermark completely vanishes!

It’s a relatively simple requirement that forces you to touch a few different areas of S/4HANA Cloud Extensibility, combining the MMPUR_PO_OUTPUT_HEADER_EXT BAdI with a little Adobe Forms JavaScript gets the job done perfectly.

Have you tackled similar form requirements in Public Cloud recently? Let me know your approach in the comments.

entry.title: Re: Create the new material type copying from HALB
entry.content:
Good day @yoshinori_kawabata33f9eb 

Thank you for your question.

Your approach in creating a custom material type by referencing HALB in the system configuration is valid.

See the following references for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f86dc2eb1f8b48c880a7607213104b27/bccab853dcfcb44ce10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US

3688161 - Standard data cannot be edited in Product Master configuration items - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

However, do not assume that the new type will behave exactly like HALB in Purchasing, MRP, and Costing unless you fully align and test your configuration. Material type behaviour depends on settings such as enabled views, quantity and value updating, account category reference, valuation related configuration, and field control.

3702849 - Error Message no. CI_DRAFTPRD_MESSAGE706 in the Manage Product Master Data (F1602) app - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

3575812 - Confirmation of Service Generated Instead of Material Document for Goods Issue - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

So, what I would suggest is:

  1. In Manage Your Solution, compare the custom type directly against HALB.
  2. Create one test product with HALB and one with the custom type, then compare available views and required fields using the Manage Product Master Data app.
  3. Thereafter, run focused tests in Purchasing, MRP, and scenarios related to Costing before you use the custom type broadly in production.
  4. Ensure you treat the custom material type as a controlled variant of HALB, not as a guaranteed one to one clone across all business processes.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Work Zone Dynamic Tile for CAP applications
entry.content:
Hi @Attila, thank you for this helpful blog!

I am facing a similar challenge and struggling with the implementation. I have opened this Q&A to summarize my case, my SAP Build Work Zone (standard edition) dynamic tile displays an "Error" message, and the request fails with a 500 Internal Server Error. When inspecting the browser's Network tab, I noticed that the KPI request is automatically routed through a URL with the following structure: ".../dynamic_dest/<destination-rt>/<AppID>/~<VersionID>~/odata/...".

<destination-rt> destination points to the HTML5 Application Repository runtime (the UI). The OData service for the KPI is exposed by my CAP backend service (<destination-srv-api>), not by the HTML5 app runtime. If I call the backend OData endpoint (by removing the "/dynamic_dest/<destination-rt>/" prefix from the URL), the service responds correctly with the expected data.

I am using a CDM deployment (cdm.json) to define my site structure and tiles.

Do you have any insights on what might be misconfigured ?

Please note that TAN-based exemptions for all suppliers can be maintained in a single step using the ''TAN Exemption Mass Upload'' application. entry.title: FUE consumption calculation with development price category
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

I'm in an implementation project of SAP S/4HANA Cloud public edition. The Starter, Dev (customizing and development tenant) and Test system has been provisioned. Production system has not been provisioned yet.

In the development tenant of dev system, I have 4 business users of development price category which consumes 8 FUE. When I check the FUE consumption in SAP for me, I see the measured value 8.

Then I go to development tenant, and remove business roles of development price category from 3 business users, and only leave 1 business user of development price category. Then the utilized value is supposed to be updated to 1, and measured value to 2.

However, when I check the FUE consumption in SAP for me again, it doesn't change. Utilized value is still 4, and measured value is still 8.

So is there any business users in other tenant also counted as utilization except in development tenant, or it's just because SAP for me has been refreshed instantly?

Thanks.

entry.title: Calibration - Refresh Subject Data Enhancement
entry.content:
If you use SAP SuccessFactors Calibration as part of your talent and performance cycles, this enhancement request may directly impact the accuracy, reliability, and admin effort of your calibration sessions.

The Challenge We’re Facing Today

In many organizations, calibration sessions are run immediately after updates are made in People Profile or Performance Management forms — such as:

  • Potential ratings
  • Performance outcomes
  • Other trend elements feeding the 9‑Box

However, once a Calibration session is activated, any updates made to these source fields are not refreshed in the session.

Even if the session is:

  • Deactivated and reactivated, or
  • Reopened after finalization

…the session continues to show stale data.

This creates a significant challenge when:

  • Talent cycles run back‑to‑back
  • People Profile is actively used as the system of record
  • Leaders expect real‑time, accurate 9‑Box positioning

Why This Is a Problem

Today, customers are forced to choose between bad options:

  • Rebuild calibration sessions from scratch
  • Manually remove and re‑add subjects
  • Accept inaccurate Potential or Performance data

This leads to:

  • Incorrect 9‑Box placement
  • Misinformed leadership discussions
  • Increased admin effort and risk during critical talent windows

In fast‑paced talent planning cycles, this simply isn’t sustainable.

The Requested Enhancement (Improvement Request #369775)

This Customer Influence request proposes a simple, powerful, and controlled solution:

✅ A “Refresh Subject Data” Capability in Calibration

Key elements of the request:

  • A Refresh Subject Data action at the session level (and optionally per subject)
  • The refresh would re‑read all mapped data sources:
    • People Profile trend elements
    • PM forms
  • No loss of:
    • In‑session comments
    • Distribution counts
    • Role assignments
  • Optional behavior:
    • Enable/disable via Manage Calibration Settings
    • Restrict who is authorized to refresh
  • Alternatively:
    • Enhance Deactivate → Activate to optionally re‑pull subject data with a confirmation prompt
  • Audit logging to track refresh actions

In short: accurate data without rebuilding sessions.

The Business Value

If implemented, this enhancement would:

  • Ensure calibration decisions are based on current data
  • Reduce admin workload and rework
  • Improve trust in 9‑Box outcomes
  • Support modern, continuous talent practices
  • Align Calibration with active People Profile usage

This is especially critical for organizations using:

  • People Profile as the single source of truth
  • Short, overlapping talent cycles
  • Advanced succession and talent reviews

Why Your Vote Matters

SAP Customer Influence enhancements are prioritized based on customer demand.

If you’ve ever:

  • Had to redo a calibration session
  • Questioned whether the 9‑Box data was truly up to date
  • Lost valuable time rebuilding sessions

…this request is for you.

👉 Please review and vote for Improvement Request #369775
Your vote helps signal how critical this capability is for the broader SuccessFactors community.

Let’s collectively improve Calibration so it works at the speed our talent decisions require.

entry.title: Comparison of Management Accounting Interfaces (CO Interfaces) in SAP S/4HANA Central Finance
entry.content:
With the release of SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS0, a new Management Accounting interface (CO interface) for Central Finance was introduced which can be used as an alternative or addition to the legacy CO interface which has been available since release SAP Simple Finance 1503. This blog provides information about the differences between both interfaces and is intended to help you to understand which interface you should use.  

To keep things simple, we use the terms, “legacy CO interface” and “modernized CO interface” in this blog.

 

 

Reasons to offer a modernized CO interface

  • The legacy CO interface was mainly built to connect ECC source systems to Central Finance. That means that it was designed on the old CO document structure (COBK/COEP). This led to disadvantages in the replication of CO documents from SAP S/4HANA source systems, for example: Challenges in replication of additional data from ACDOCA
  • Challenges in replication of different field lengths in ACDOCA (e.g. quantities and amounts)
  • No support of replication of custom field extensions in ACDOCA
  • No consideration of additional FI currencies in replication of CO documents
  • Innovations in SAP S/4HANA, such as Universal Parallel Accounting and Attributed CO-PA were not considered and cannot be supported out-of-the-box

 

Furthermore, the legacy CO interface has disadvantages when it comes to  technical replication. The legacy CO interface is based on inserting data into the COBK table in the source system(s). However, this kind of triggering of the replication of CO documents is not efficient: All CO business transactions are sent to SLT and filtering is carried out in SLT to eliminate the irrelevant transactions. This increases both data volume and traffic. A cleaner interface is therefore desirable.

The creation of a modernized, dedicated CO interface for SAP S/4HANA source systems aims to solve all these issues while offering a future-proof solution which supports new developments in SAP S/4HANA in the area of Management Accounting.

 

Overview of main differences between legacy and modernized CO Interface

 

 ​

Legacy CO Interface​

Modernized CO Interface

Designed for​

ECC source systems

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition source systems

​Prerequisite: Supported Releases

​From SAP Simple Finance, on-premise edition 1503 (CFIN)

From S/4HANA 2025 FPS0 (source and CFIN release)

Prerequisite: Supported Profitability Analysis

Depending on release, see SAP Note 2320298

Margin Analysis active in source and CFIN system

Data Trigger

Table COBK

Table CFIN_S4CO_STG_HD

AIF Interface

CO_DOC

S4CO_DOC

Extension BAdI

BADI_FINS_CFIN_CO_INTERFACE

BADI_FINS_CFIN_S4CO_DOC_INTF

Data Volume

High data traffic as all business transactions are sent to SLT and then filtered out in SLT

Lower traffic as only supported business transactions are sent to SLT

Granularity

Lower granularity as based on COBK/COEP

High granularity as based on ACDOCA

Supported CO Business Transactions

See detailed comparison below

See detailed comparison below

 

 Comparison of supported CO Business Transactions

 

CO Business Transaction

Description

Legacy CO Interface

Modernized CO Interface

Remark

GPDP

Distribution Primary Costs

x

 

Industry solution, deprecated in SAP S/4HANA

GPDS

Distribution Secondary Costs

x

 

Industry solution, deprecated in SAP S/4HANA

JRIU

JV-Seg.adjustm.assessment

x

x

 

JRIV

JV-Seg.adjustm.distribution

x

x

 

JVIU

JV Actual assessment

x

x

 

JVIV

JV Actual distribution

x

x

 

KAMV

Manual Cost Allocation

x

x

 

KAZI

Actual cost center accrual

x

x

 

KOAL

Actual Settlement of Internal Activity Allocation

x

x

 

KOAO

Actual Settlement (Not for CO-PA scenario)

x

x

 

KOLI

Collective order delivery

x

 

Not supported

KSI0

Actual Cost Center Split

x

 

Old transaction, deprecated in SAP S/4HANA

KSI1

Actual Split Costs (Primary)

x

 

Old transaction, deprecated in SAP S/4HANA

KSI2

Actual Split Costs (Secondary)

x

 

Old transaction, deprecated in SAP S/4HANA

KSI3

Actual Split Costs Internal Activity Allocation

x

 

Old transaction, deprecated in SAP S/4HANA

KSII

Actual Price Calculation

x

x

 

KSPA

Assessment to CO-PA

x

x

 

KTDA

COPA: TOP-DOWN: Actuals

x

 

Replaced by Universal Allocation in SAP S/4HANA

KZPI

Actual Overhead (Periodic)

x

x

 

KZPR

Actual Overhead (Realtime)

x

x

 

RKIB

Actual Periodic Repostings

x

x

 

RKIL

Actual Inverse Activity Allocation

x

x

 

RKIU

Actual Overhead Assessment

x

x

 

RKIV

Actual Overhead Distribution

x

x

 

RKN

Actual non-alloc. activities

x

 

Not relevant in S/4HANA Private Cloud strategy

RKL

Actual Activity Allocation

x

x

 

RKLN

Revaluation at Actual Price

x

x

 

RKLT

Actual Template Allocation

x

x

 

RKLX

Predistribution of fixed costs

x

 

Not relevant in S/4HANA Private Cloud strategy

RKS

Statistical Key Figures

x

 

Not supported

RKU1

Repost Costs

x

x

 

RKU2

Repost revenue

x

x

 

RKU3

Repost CO line items

x

x

 

RRIB

Seg. adjust.: Act.per.repost.

x

x

 

RRIU

Segment adjust.: Act. Assess.

x

x

 

RRIV

Segment adjust.: Act. distrib.

x

x

 

 

 

Usage Recommendation

  • Customers who only have ECC source systems: continue using the legacy CO Interface
  • Customers who only have SAP S/4HANA source systems: if prerequisites are met, start using the modernized CO Interface
  • Customers with a mix of ECC and SAP S/4HANA source systems: deeper analysis necessary. Highly dependent  on which CO business transactions are used/needed. Parallel use of legacy and redesigned CO Interface can be considered.

 

How to move from legacy to modernized CO interface

If you have already replicated CO documents using the legacy CO interface and start now using the modernized CO interface for the same source system you must use either legacy interface CO_DOC or modernized interface S4CO_DOC per controlling area to avoid postings being replicated twice.

Therefore, when you start using S4CO_DOC you must ensure that in CO_DOC you filter out postings for all company codes belonging to a controlling area that has been specified in the customizing for S4CO_DOC. To do so, you need to use filters in SLT. Additional information about the filters can be found in the attachment Filter Rules for Central Finance in SAP Note 2154420.

The following steps are necessary to set-up the modernized CO Interface:

  • Configuration in the source system
    • Company Codes for ongoing replication
    • Characteristics in Margin Analysis relevant for replication
  • Set-Up SLT
    • Install SLT content
    • Define the replication object
    • Apply the SLT content
  • Configuration in the CFIN system
    • Configure AIF with the new AIF content

 

Summary

If you want to connect SAP S/4HANA source systems to your Central Finance system, the modernized CO Interface is the best option. Benefits of using the modernized CO Interface are:

  • Future-proof – new SAP S/4HANA functionalities can be supported​
  • ​Replication of additional information from ACDOCA
  • Low effort to implement the modernized CO Interface ​
  • Reduction in replicated data volume ​
  • Performance improvements

Before moving to the modernized CO Interface, a proper analysis must be done to ensure all prerequisites and requirements are met.

 

Related SAP Notes:

Legacy CO Interface

Main Note: https://me.sap.com/notes/2103482

FAQ Note: https://me.sap.com/notes/2959532

CO-PA related postings: https://me.sap.com/notes/2320298

 

Modernized CO Interface

Main Note: https://me.sap.com/notes/3619137

entry.title: Your AI Models Just Got a Hardware Upgrade
entry.content:
Co-Author: Lukas Pätz

TL;DR

SAP AI Core (the engine) now supports flexible instance types — pick the exact CPU or GPU your workload needs
New instance types deliver up to 4.5–5x the compute performance of old resource plans
Access the latest GPUs (L4, L40S, H100) without waiting for SAP to package them into plans
Your existing resource plans still work — instance types are opt-in where they help

Start Here: SAP Note 3660109

Why This Matters

If you're running your custom AI models on SAP's Generative AI Hub (technical service SAP AI Core), this one's for you.

We've heard the feedback: newer GPUs are available from hyperscalers, but you can't use them yet because SAP hasn't released a matching resource plan. That lag is frustrating — especially when the difference between a V100 and an H100 isn't incremental. It's generational. We're talking 4.5–5x the compute performance for the right workloads. An inference call that takes 8 seconds on older hardware might finish in under 1 second on newer chips. That's the gap between "possible in theory" and "shipping in production."

Starting now, that gap is closing.
 

What Changed: Resource Plans → Instance Types

Think of the old model like a set menu at a restaurant. Curated, predictable, but limited to what the chef decided to offer.

Instance types are the shift to à la carte. You see the full range of CPUs and GPUs available across cloud providers, and you pick exactly what your workload needs — from a lightweight CPU for preprocessing to an H100 for training large models.

Resource plans aren't going away. They still work. But instance types are now the primary path for getting the newest, most capable hardware into your deployments — faster, and with finer control than the old model allowed. We highly recommend to leverage instance types going forward (only available in the "extended" service plan, now be the right time to migrate from standard to extended)

Here's the point: you tell us what your model needs; we make sure the right compute is there. Bon appétit.

What You Actually Get

A quick clarification: If you're using pre-integrated foundation models through SAP's Generative AI Hub — GPT-5.2, Claude 4.6 Opus or Gemini — this update doesn't directly affect you. Those models are already managed and optimized; you just call the API.

Instance types matter when you're running your own models: custom fine-tuned models, your proprietary models (like team-built solutions), embedding models for RAG pipelines, or specialized computer vision and industry models. That's when hardware choice becomes your concern — and when better hardware translates directly into better outcomes.

Faster inference, faster training

This isn't about spec sheets. It's about what becomes possible with the models you've built.

Custom models that were borderline viable for real-time use — domain-specific classifiers, fine-tuned embedding models, proprietary prediction models — become clearly viable when you can run them on L40S instead of T4. Training jobs that took days can finish in hours on H100. Features your team considered out of reach might now fit inside your existing budget.

L4 and L40S chips deliver substantially higher throughput than T4, at lower cost per inference call. H100 provides order-of-magnitude gains over V100 for training workloads. If you're pushing the limits of what's feasible with custom models, this matters.

Better cost efficiency — without re-architecting

Faster hardware means less compute time per job, which means lower cost. Simple as that.

Instance types also let you right-size. You stop paying for a resource plan heavier than you need. The result: better cost-per-output without touching your model or application logic.

 

New hardware when it ships — not months later

When hyperscalers release the next GPU generation, you won't be waiting for SAP to define a new resource plan. Instance types let us surface new hardware significantly faster. Your infrastructure stays current.

Multi-cloud, multi-region resilience

Instance types are available across cloud providers and regions. Deploy closer to your users or data. Meet compliance requirements. Reduce single-vendor dependency. Keep two or three validated alternatives per region and you're protected against capacity constraints.

Which Instance Should You Use?

 

WorkloadRecommended InstanceExamples
Orchestration, preprocessing, CPU-based MLCPU (AWS m7i, Azure Dv6, GCP n4)Data pipelines, API routing, lightweight model serving
Custom model inference (high throughput)L4 / L40S GPU (AWS g6, g6e)Fine-tuned classifiers, embedding models, custom vision models
Fine-tuning and training medium-to-large modelsA100 (GCP A2 Ultra)Domain-specific model fine-tuning, complex custom inference
Training large language modelsH100 (Azure NC H100 v5)Custom LLM training, proprietary foundation model development

Match the instance to your workload. Account for regional availability. Keep at least two alternatives validated per region.

Use the compute factors in SAP Note 3660109 or the SAP Cost Calculator to compare options on a consistent basis — each instance type maps to a compute factor in node-hours, which makes cost planning straightforward.

Migration: No Big Bang Required

Both options work today. You don't have to migrate everything at once. More details:

# This still works
ai.sap.com/resourcePlan: "infer.s"

# This is the new option — use where it helps
ai.sap.com/instanceType: "g6.4xlarge"

Start with the workloads where newer hardware will have the clearest impact — typically your highest-volume inference jobs or your most compute-heavy training runs. Everything else can stay on resource plans, but newer hardware might be more efficient.

Full specs, regional availability, and compute factors: SAP Note 3660109.

Staying Current

Hardware in AI is moving fast — faster than enterprise software typically keeps up. Instance types are how we keep you on the right side of that curve, without adding operational overhead to your team.

To stay informed:

  • Subscribe to SAP Note 3660109 — it's the live catalogue; new instance types show up here as they become available
  • Watch the What's New page for implementation guidance and new instance families

The Bottom Line

This isn't a small tweak. It's a deliberate shift to put more control in your hands — and to get it there faster.

We handle the complexity: evaluating new hardware, qualifying it, making it available at scale. You focus on what you're actually here to do: build AI solutions that matter.

The menu just expanded. Pick your instance — and dig in.

entry.title: Re: What Is Agentic AI and Why SAP Teams Are Paying Attention in 2026
entry.content: Tks for the very interesting comments.  A practical point from a procurement and supply chain perspective is that these concepts only work well if the underlying SAP data is stable. In many cases, the real issue is not the architecture itself, but inconsistent master data or incomplete transaction history. This leads to results that look correct technically but are not reliable in daily operations. A common pitfall is assuming that governance or guardrails can fix these issues. In reality, they can only control decisions within the limits of the available data. It is often more effective to first ensure consistent vendor, material, and demand data, and clearly define how decisions are made in the process. Once this foundation is stable, more advanced approaches can be introduced with much lower risk

entry.title: Re: Skipped Set Up Organizational Structure?how can I fix...
entry.content:
Good day @yeziyimm8 

Thank you for your question.

Question 1: Since CBC is not configured yet, can I directly delete Company Code A in the customizing environment if it has no related configurations?

Answer: The safe answer is no. You should not expect to directly delete Code A from the tenant just because CBC has not yet been completed. Organisation entities like company codes are not designed for you to be able to delete them in this setup and the usual fallback is for you to stop using the entry and mark it clearly. Maybe with DO NOT USE. See 3580500 - Deletion of organizational units in the SSCUI 100066 - SAP for Me for more details.

Question 2: After deactivating it in the customizing environment, can I start over and configure Company Code A in the CBC environment, then activate and use it in the customizing environment? Or do I have to use a new Company Code B?

Answer: Also, you deactivating Company Code A does not reliably reset the ID for you to be able to reuse it in CBC. Note that reusing the same ID can still fail because the organisational unit identity already exists, and you manually customising changes can become inconsistent with CBC managed organisational structure.

3437598 - Company deletion is not possible in SAP Central Business Configuration - SAP for Me

3591436 - Unable to assign the purchase organization to company code XXXX - SAP for Me

What I suggest you should do/consider is:

  • Rename or decommission Company Code A operationally if needed, instead of trying to remove it technically.
  • In CBC, create a new company code ID and continue your setup there.
  • Going forward, use CBC as your sources for setting up your organisational structure instead of manually creating core organisation units first in the customising environment.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Creating Production Order Component Line Items via API
entry.content: You can find the documentation on the SAP API Hub (https://api.sap.com/bointerface/I_PRODUCTIONORDERTP). If you search for 'component' in the object I_PRODUCTIONORDERTP you'll find the documentation about the create operation including example code.

entry.title: Digital Procurement Transformation: A Modern Guide for Buying Organisations
entry.content:
Most organisations still recognise the familiar rhythm of traditional procurement: emails, spreadsheets, slow approvals, and a daily effort to keep things moving. It works—until it doesn’t. When a supplier fails, when spend balloons, or when a last‑minute request arrives, the gaps become visible.

Now imagine an alternative. A workplace where teams request what they need through a clear, guided process. Approvals move quietly in the background. Supplier information stays accurate without manual chasing. Leaders see spend in real time, not weeks later. This is the world that digital procurement transformation aims to create.

The transformation is not only technical. It is behavioural. It changes how organisations coordinate, decide, and respond. And when done well, it turns procurement into a steady, strategic partner.

What Digital Procurement Transformation Means in Practice

Digital procurement transformation replaces disconnected, manual activity with connected systems and evidence‑based decisions. It shifts procurement from a collection of tasks to a coherent operational system.

Three changes define the shift:

  1. Digital workflows instead of manual routines.
    Tasks that once required phone calls and spreadsheets move into structured, traceable processes.

  2. Real‑time visibility instead of retrospective reporting.
    Data becomes immediate and actionable, allowing teams to anticipate rather than react.

  3. A connected procurement environment.
    Procurement, finance, IT, and suppliers operate within the same digital landscape rather than separate channels.

This is where platforms such as SAP S/4HANA and SAP Ariba often play a role. Many organisations use them to bring sourcing, contracting, purchasing, and invoicing into a single ecosystem. The value does not come from the tool alone but from the coherence it helps create. When all procurement activity flows through one environment, people work with greater confidence and fewer surprises.

 Digital procurement is therefore not a destination. It is a shift in quality: clarity, speed, and trust.

Why Organisations Are Making the Shift Now

Three forces make digital procurement transformation more important than ever.

1. Manual processes no longer scale

Lengthy approvals, repeat data entry, and unclear spend categories all slow the organisation down. Errors accumulate. Opportunities are missed. In contrast, digital processes reduce friction and allow procurement teams to focus on value rather than administration.

2. Supply chains are more fragile

Disruptions—from storms to shortages—arrive without warning. Organisations relying on manual visibility struggle to respond. Digital systems help procurement spot early signals and assess alternative suppliers before risks escalate.

3. Expectations are rising

Most employees now expect consumer‑grade simplicity in workplace systems. Leaders want visibility they can trust. Finance wants control without slowing the business. Digital procurement is often the only way to meet all of these expectations simultaneously.

The result is a shift in identity. Procurement becomes a partner in planning, risk, and strategy—not just in purchasing.

How Organisations Transform Successfully

Digital procurement transformation works best when the organisation builds momentum through clear intent and consistent behaviour.

Set a Clear, Shared Vision

Teams need to know what the transformation is there to achieve. Is the goal faster buying? Better compliance? Improved planning? A reduction in maverick spend? Clear aims align decisions and make trade‑offs easier.

Secure Visible Leadership Support

Support from the CPO, CFO, and CIO provides direction and stability. Leaders help remove barriers and reinforce the message that new ways of working are here to stay.

Choose Technology That Fits the Organisation

The ideal digital platform supports the way the organisation works. It should integrate with finance, guide users through simple choices, and scale as needs evolve.

This is where systems like SAP Ariba often help. For example:

  • Organisations use SAP Ariba Guided Buying to give employees a simple, intuitive entry point—reducing training needs and cutting errors.
  • Ariba Supplier Lifecycle and Performance helps centralise supplier onboarding and oversight, reducing the scattered email chains that many organisations struggle with.
  • Through SAP Ariba Contracts, teams create and store contracts in a single place, improving visibility and reducing version confusion.

These are not features for the sake of features. They address common organisational problems: fragmented information, inconsistent processes, and slow decisions.

Support People Through the Change

Technology changes quickly. Habits do not.
Training, communication, and early involvement of staff make the transition smoother. When users understand what improves for them—fewer errors, fewer steps, clearer rules—they are more willing to adopt new systems.

There is often a moment of hesitation here. A new workflow might feel slower the first time. A button is in a different place. The familiar shortcut no longer works. This moment is normal. It marks the start of new habits forming, not a flaw in the transformation.

Monitor Progress and Adjust

Digital procurement is not a one‑time installation. Organisations refine processes over time. Reviewing cycle times, automation levels, and compliance rates helps identify where improvements will have the greatest effect.

Small adjustments, made steadily, create lasting change.

Common Challenges and How to Navigate Them

Even well‑run programmes face predictable hurdles.

Unclear Goals

Without specific objectives, teams struggle to prioritise. Clear targets help everyone understand what matters most.

Resistance to Change

People worry about losing control or becoming less relevant. Involving them early and showing how the new approach reduces daily frustration builds trust.

Poor Data Quality

Digital tools rely on accurate information. Cleaning supplier records, contract metadata, and spend categories before implementation prevents downstream issues.

SAP Ariba customers often highlight how centralised data standards help: once suppliers update their details in the Ariba Network, those changes flow through automatically rather than relying on manual correction.

Integration Complexity

Connecting procurement with finance, operations, and planning can be challenging. Collaboration with IT and careful testing reduce the risk of breaks between systems.

Addressing these challenges early keeps momentum intact and avoids the spiral of workarounds that can undermine new systems.

What a Successful Transformation Looks Like

A strong digital procurement transformation brings measurable and behavioural improvements.

Faster Buying

Routine purchases complete quickly. Users rely on guided catalogues. Approvals flow without manual intervention.

Better Cost Control

Spend becomes visible and predictable. Procurement teams spot patterns and work with suppliers to achieve better value.

Greater Transparency

Data becomes reliable. Leaders see where money goes. Compliance improves naturally because systems guide people towards correct choices.

More Collaborative Supplier Relationships

Digital platforms make it easier to share information and address issues early.
Many organisations use SAP Ariba Supplier Management to standardise onboarding steps, track performance, and maintain a shared audit trail.

Scalability and Adaptability

Cloud‑based systems evolve with the business. Teams can add new categories, suppliers, or processes without major disruption.

These improvements take hold gradually. The first few weeks may feel unfamiliar. By the third or fourth month, teams begin to recognise the rhythm of the new process. Over time, the earlier manual world feels distant.

A Practical Example: A manufacturer in Construction & Engineering

A large manufacturer in Construction & Engineering faced slow cycle times, limited visibility, and inconsistent supplier management. Most processes were manual. Only a small share of spend was under contract. Approvals often stalled in inboxes.

The company introduced digital procurement step by step. It used guided buying principles, automated purchase orders, and AI‑supported spend insights. Supplier onboarding moved from scattered emails to a structured digital flow. Training focused on helping teams feel confident early.

Within a year, procurement cycle times fell sharply. Most purchase orders were automated. Supplier delivery performance improved. Compliance rose. And users—initially sceptical—reported that buying had become easier.

The interesting shift was cultural. Procurement was no longer a bottleneck. It became a source of clarity and consistency.

The Role of SAP Ariba in the Wider Journey

Digital procurement transformation can succeed with various tools, but SAP Ariba often appears in organisations seeking scalability and integration. Its value lies not in its features alone but in how it supports fundamental behaviours:

  • Clarity through intuitive guided buying.
  • Control through centralised contracts and supplier data.
  • Connection through shared processes that link procurement, finance, and suppliers.

These capabilities help organisations build consistent habits. When people trust the system, they stop inventing workarounds. And when teams share a coherent digital environment, they make better decisions together.

If SAP Ariba were removed, the principles of digital procurement transformation would remain. But with it, many organisations find those principles easier to put into daily practice.

A Return to the Opening Image

At the start, we imagined a workplace where buying is simple, transparent, and dependable. That world can feel distant, especially when teams are navigating spreadsheets and long approval chains.

Digital procurement transformation does not make that world appear overnight. But with clear goals, steady leadership, good technology, and patient adoption, the organisation edges closer each day.

There comes a moment—often quiet—when someone places an order, tracks its status, and completes their work without needing to chase anyone. And the team realises that the future they pictured at the start is no longer imagined. It is simply how procurement works now.

Kind regards

Lars Elmquist

entry.title: Delay one operation of task in maintenance plan
entry.content:

I created a task with multiple operations.

All operations have a weekly cycle, but one of them has a 6 week cycle.

Now I want to create multiple maintenance plans which reference this task; my problem is, if I launch them at the same time, every maintenance plan will bring up the 6 week cycle operation on the same week.

Right now I see one option: launch the plans with a week delay each, this way the 6 week cycle operation will have a week delay aswell (compared to the other maintenance plans)

But is there a way to delay the operation itself when launching the plan? This way I could launch all the plans at once, but that one operation will have a delay.

Thanks in advance, I hope my question is understandable

entry.title: Re: AI driven Composite Creation in SAP Analytics Cloud using SAP AI Core
entry.content:
Hi @zee_siddique15 ,

The reason the Date field is being treated as a measure is because the values (like 202601–202652) are in a numeric format. The system is interpreting them as numbers rather than a date dimension.

To fix this, you can try formatting the Date column as a proper date (or even as a string/categorical field) before uploading, this should make it recognize it correctly as a dimension.

entry.title: Re: Few important app is not available - FB01
entry.content:
Hi Brijesh,

There are two issues in your question- 

First one- Vendor1 credit and Vendor 2 debit or vice versa.

iam nt sure what is the business case that you will offset one vendor with other, This does not seem to be appropriate. But still if you want to do, you cannot do it in SAP directly. You need to use one Intermediary GL account (or clearing account) and post two entries in system. 

Entry one: Debit Intermediary GL account; credit Vendor 1

Entry two: credit Intermediary GL account; debit Vendor 2

Second case: Debot 1 GL account and credit 3 vendors Account. This also does not seem to be appropriate business case. Vendors invoice are pisted based on the PO or FI invoice submitted by vendor. Three vendors  submitting  one invoice look weird. Please recheck the business requirement again. If still you want to post, u cannot do it in one document. You need to do it as three invoices in S4 HANA.

entry.title: In the Valuation Balance app, rent increase does not update succeeding opening/closing balances
entry.content:
In the Valuation Balance app (Cash Flow Data for Valuation) report, rent increase/decrease does not update succeeding opening/closing balances.

When increasing the rent in a lease contract, the Valuation Balance app only shows the adjusted opening/ending balances of the asset/liability but does not highlight the change. This leads to the opening and closing balances not being the same in the subsequent month after the change.

Can we have a report where remeasurements like this are included?

• Lease asset: opening balance + remeasurement – depreciation = ending balance
• Lease liability: opening balance + remeasurement (should be equal to the amount in the lease asset) – payment + interest accretion = ending balance


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: In the Valuation Balance app, rent increase does not...
entry.content:
Thank you for your question!

The App, 'Valuation Balance - Information System' is capable of displaying the contract balances determined via the valuation performed. It does not account for the values impacting the balance change separately, but rather includes them in the overall balance - with some exceptions for liability difference postings, initial costs, retroactive changes etc.

If you would like to review the overall valuation flow which would also display in detail all the changes leading to the adjustment of the opening/closing balances, I can recommend utilizing App 'Display Contract Valuation' that highlights these entries as well.

In addition, you can explore building custom reports fit to your specific business requirement by exploring the released and available CDS views for Contract and Lease Management.

entry.title: AI capabilities for sanctioned party list screening within SAP GTS Edition for HANA
entry.content:
The SPL screening accuracy of GTS has improvement potentials. Official Lists of denied parties are growing and we have more feeder systems which require screening. 

AI will have to be implemented to go through the large amounts of SPL hits where over 90 % of both Business partners and documents are false positives. 

Benefit: avoid disturbing business unnecessarily. Automatic release of business partners, orders, deliveries and payments which are blocked due to false positives to reduce business handling time (within predefined confidence level).

entry.title: Announcing the First-Ever System-Based SAP Customer Data Platform Certification
entry.content:
On behalf of the Product Learning team at SAP, I am excited to introduce a new way to validate your SAP Customer Data Platform skills: a system-based certification that measures your ability to perform real tasks and scenarios in SAP Customer Data Platform. Instead of selecting answers on a theory-only exam, you’ll complete hands-on activities in an SAP-provisioned environment—demonstrating the day-to-day capabilities that matter on live sites. Full details and the latest policies are available on the certification page:

SAP Certified - Implementation Consultant - SAP Customer Data Platform

 

🌟A New Era of Practical, System-Based Assessment

Unlike standard multiple-choice exams, this new certification provides candidates with:
  • A SAP Customer Data Platform system, and
  • A PDF containing a set of realistic tasks to complete
Candidates perform actual configuration and scenario-based exercises directly in the system. Once all required tasks are completed, they can submit their results for automated evaluation. Within minutes, candidates will know whether they have passed or failed — offering a faster, more authentic, and more meaningful way to validate capability.
This practical approach ensures that certified consultants are not only knowledgeable but also skilled in applying SAP Customer Data Platform functionality in realistic customer scenarios.

🎯Why System Based Certifications?

The reimagined SAP Certification exams offer a range of benefits. More specifically:

  • Job-ready proof: Demonstrates you can navigate SAP Customer Data Platform and execute business-critical tasks—not just recall concepts.
  • Strong SAP signal: An SAP-recognised credential and digital badge that hiring managers and project leads across the ecosystem trust.
  • Practice over memorisation: Preparation aligns to SAP Learning Journeys and hands-on exercises for better retention and impact.
  • Real-world relevance: Validates cross-functional workflows (catalogue, content, search, promotions) that move conversion and revenue KPIs.
  • Always current: Content and recertification align with SAP’s cloud release cycle, reflecting the latest features and UI. 
  • Higher integrity than Q&A-only: Performance on practical tasks reduces guesswork and emphasises authentic ability.

👤 Who Is This Certification For?

This certification is designed for:

  • Consultants

It is classified as an intermediate-level certification and requires approximately 3 hours to complete.

📚How to Prepare

To set yourself up for success, we recommend the following preparation path:

1. Complete the "Implementing SAP Customer Data Platform" Learning Journey

The Learning Journey for Consultants on learning.sap.com provides the foundational knowledge required for the hands-on portion of the certification. They combine structured courses with access to practice systems for guided exercises, building familiarity with the interfaces, workflows, and configuration steps assessed.

2. (Optional) Attend the In-Person Classroom Trainings

For learners who prefer guided instruction, tan intructional led training is available:

  • SAP Customer Data Platform Implementation

This class provides expert-led, structured instruction and practice to deepen your understanding and skills.

 

📢Spread the Word!

Help us share this exciting update with partners, colleagues, and other stakeholders.

📝We Value Your Feedback

After completing the certification, please take a moment to share your feedback. Your insights help us continuously improve the learning experience.
 
Two easy ways to share feedback!
  1. Click “Share Feedback” to submit your thoughts. 

     

     

  2. In the “Notification Center” under “Learning” you can select the congratulatory email after completing course or learning journey and click “Feedback” to share your thoughts.
