entry.title: Law 9/2025 for Spain: The Digital Administrative Control Document for Road Transport
entry.content:
Spain's Law 9/2025 on Sustainable Mobility, published in December 2025, mandates that the Administrative Control Document (Documento de Control Administrativo, DCA) — a paper-based document required for every public road freight shipment — must become fully digital within 10 months of the law's entry into force. This means the Electronic Administrative Control Document (eDCA) becomes mandatory by approximately October 2026. In this post, we break down what this means, how eCMR fits in, and the two compliance methods available to companies.
 

The Administrative Control Document (DCA)

The Administrative Control Document (Documento de Control Administrativo, DCA) is regulated by Order FOM/2861/2012. It is a mandatory document required for every public road transportation of goods within Spain.

Key Characteristics

  • Scope: Applicable only in Spain, for every shipment of goods by road.
  • Copies: According to Article 8, two copies must be issued — one for the loader company and one for the carrier.
  • Format: The document has no regulated format (no official template), but must contain all mandatory information.

Mandatory Information (Articles 5 and 6)

The DCA must contain, at minimum, the following information:

  • Loader (Company code). Company Name, Tax ID Number (NIF/CIF), and Fiscal Address
  • Carrier (Transporter). Company Name and Tax ID Number (NIF/CIF)
  • Place of Origin. Starting point of the merchandise transport
  • Place of Destination. Delivery point of the merchandise
  • Nature of Merchandise. Description/type of goods being transported
  • Weight. Weight of the transported merchandise
  • Date of Transport. Date when the transport takes place
  • Vehicle License Plate(s). All license plates must be reported. For articulated vehicles, all plates are required. If the vehicle changes during transport, the carrier must update this information.
  • Observations. Notes, remarks, and any other relevant information

Other Transport Documents

Understanding the DCA requires context about other transport documents in the Spanish and European landscape:

     National Consignment Note (Carta de Porte Nacional)

An optional document regulated by Order FOM/1882/2012. It records the contract details of road transport. Applicable only in Spain.

    CMR (Carta de Porte — International)

Spain is a signatory of the CMR Convention (Convenio relativo al Contrato de Transporte Internacional de Mercancías por Carretera). The CMR document serves as probative evidence of the international road transport contract. It establishes the relationship between the loader and transporter and is used as evidence of the reception of goods.

The CMR is only required for international road transports where:
  • Goods are loaded in Country A and delivered in Country B.
  • At least one of the countries is a signatory of the CMR Convention

    eCMR — The Electronic CMR

The eCMR is the electronic version of the CMR document. Spain adhered to the eCMR protocol in 2011 (Instrumento de Adhesión de España al Protocolo Adicional al Convenio relativo al contrato de transporte internacional de mercancías por carretera).

At the European level, EU Regulation 2020/1056 on electronic freight transport information (eFTI) establishes common rules for the electronic exchange of information between operators and administrations. In this context, eCMR is a key tool for the modernization of road transport through electronic means.

The CMR and eCMR documents are accepted as a valid Administrative Control Document, as long as all the mandatory information listed above is included.
 

Law 9/2025 "Ley de Movilidad Sostenible"

The Law 9/2025 on Sustainable Mobility , published in December 2025, introduces a critical mandate in its Transient Provision Eight:

  • Digitalization of the DCA (= eDCA)
  • Mandatory Digital DCA by October 2026
  • The Administrative Control Document (Order FOM/2861/2012) must necessarily be digital 10 months after the entry into force of Law 9/2025 (October 2026)

The Ministry of Transports provides two methods to meet this requirement, published on its official website (Documento de Control Digital)

 

 

eDCA storage in the Ministry-Managed Repository

The company submits the eDCA to the Ministry of Transport on demand, upon inspection request during transport. The document is identified by an assigned code. ("Remisión de documento de control")

  • File format: PDF/A-1a
  • Maximum file size: 4 MB per file
  • The control document must be identified by the code assigned in the transport control

This process is exemplified in this video by the Ministry of Transports: Documento de Control Digital y aplicacion para su gestion. 

 

eDCA storage in the Company-Managed Repository

The company stores the eDCA on its own servers and includes a QR code embedded in the document pointing to the original document's URL. Inspectors scan the QR to access it. ("Comunicación de dominio de plataformas de documentos de control electrónicos"). Pre-requisites:

  • The EDCA must be stored in a registered repository in PDF format.
  • The company must have previously registered its storage domain with the Ministry of Transport.
  • The domain must use an HTTPS connection.
  • The QR code must point to the original eDCA document on the registered domain.

 

Electronic Signature Requirements

According to Article 4 of Order FOM/2861/2012, the Administrative Control Document must be formalized (signed) by:

  • The effective carrier company (company owning the transport authorization).
  • The contractual loader company (company that contracts the carrier for the shipment).

The Resolution by the General Directorate of Road Transports (May 22, 2023) establishes that the EDCA can be used digitally only if specific conditions are met. These conditions align with Article 26 of the eIDAS Regulation on Advanced Electronic Signatures:

  • The signature must be uniquely linked to the relevant signatory
  • The signature must allow the identification of the signatory
  • Created using means that the signatory can keep under their sole control
  • Linked to the signed data so that any subsequent modification can be detected
❌The following are NOT valid as electronic signatures:
  • Handwritten signature images inserted in the PDF document
  • Acceptance checkboxes to click
  • Any digital signatures that do not meet the Advanced Electronic Signature criteria
 

Persistence

According to Article 9 of Order FOM/2861/2012:

  • The Administrative Control Document (or a copy of it) must be kept available for inspection for at least 1 year.
  • Any kind of storage can be used, as long as the required information remains readable.
  • The eDCA stored in the Company-Managed Repository, the storage location must be accessible from outside via a URL on a registered HTTPS domain.

 

Disclaimer

This blog only intends to explain the new regulatory aspects of Law 9/2025 (Ley de Movilidad Sostenible) regarding the Administrative Control Document.
SAP is currently analyzing this requirement . To stay up to date with the latest news about this analysis, check the publication "Law 9/2025 on Sustainable Mobility: Administrative Control Document" in the Regulatory Change Manager. The final assessment of this feature will determine whether it will be rejected, or the scope approved for development.

This blog post reflects the author's interpretation of Spanish legal requirements and SAP system behavior as of the publication date. It is intended for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal advice. For specific guidance, consult your legal or tax advisor and refer to official SAP documentation.

The links to external sources in this blog may be modified at any time externally by the owner without previous notice.

entry.title: Re: How to set up MaxDB for JDBC connection via TLS/SSL
entry.content:
Hi,

the securing of java connection consists of 3 steps:

1. Create server private security environment file (PSE)

2. Import the certificate to the java client

3. enter encryption property into connection url

In the following description we assume the MaxDB global Programs is located in /sapdb/programs, Global Data is located in /sapdb/data. You can find these settings in /etc/opt/sdb

Steps for configuration

Prerequisites

Installation of SAP Cryptographic Library CommonCryptoLib

(s. Installing of SAP Cryptographic library in SAP help)

In SAP Java J2EE system the corresponding library, libsapcrypto.so, is in $DIR_INSTANCE/exe directory. I.e.  (/usr/sap/<SID>/J<NR>/exe).

Certificates configuration on Server side

Make SAP cryptographic library available on SAP MaxDB data base host in the global programs lib directory (/sapdb/programs/lib), change ownership and permissions according to other modules in this directory.

Example:

chown sdb:sdba /sapdb/programs/lib/libsapcrypto.so

chmod 755 /sapdb/programs/lib/libsapcrypto.so

 

Generate MaxDB Server PSE file

Certificate can be created either with sapgenpse tool, which is part of SAP J2EE installation or using SAP Web Dispatcher (s. SAP help).

Example for sapgenpse usage:

Set environment

export  SECUDIR=/sapdb/data/sec

export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/sapdb/programs/lib:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH

Create PSE file

sudo -u sdb /usr/sap/<SID>/J<NR>/exe/sapgenpse gen_pse -p SDBSSLS.pse -x "" -noreq "CN=localhost"

You can use further specifications for the PSE creation, however identifier "CN=localhost" is important, because there is hostname check.

Result: the PSE file /sapdb/data/sec/SDBSSLS.pse is created

Export certificate

Login as OS user <sid>adm and do the steps:

export SECUDIR=/sapdb/data/sec

/usr/sap/<SID>/J<NR>/exe/sapgenpse export_own_cert -o /sapdb/data/sec/SDBSSLS.crt -p SDBSSLS.pse

Result: the certificate file /sapdb/data/sec/SDBSSLS.crt is created.

Add MaxDB certificate to the J2EE secure store

Global secure store

As OS user <sid>adm execute the command:

keytool -import -alias "maxdblistener" -file /sapdb/data/sec/SDBSSLS.crt -keystore /usr/sap/<SID>/SYS/exe/jvm/linuxx86_64/sapjvm_8.1.091/sapjvm_8/jre/lib/security/cacerts

The tool keytool will ask for the password. If you didn’t customize it, the initial value is ‘changeit’.

Local secure store

keytool -import -alias "maxdblistener" -file /sapdb/data/sec/SDBSSLS.crt -keystore /usr/sap/<SID>/J<NR>/exe/sapjvm_8/jre/lib/security/cacerts

 

Restart MaxDB communication listener

Extend LD_LIBRARY_PATH with the path of libsapcrypto :

(normally it is already in the environment of the j2ee engine user, like /usr/sap/<SID>/J<NR>/exe/libsapcrypto.so)

Stop listener

/sapdb/programs/bin/sdbgloballistener -all stop

Start listener

/sapdb/programs/bin/sdbgloballistener

 

Check in the global working directory that SSL server was started too

cat /sapdb/data/wrk/nisslserver_<host>.prot_7270

 

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Date       Time       PID   TID Typ  MsgID Label    Message Text

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

2022-12-13 12:55:02 18526 28736 INF  12945 NISSLSRV U64/LIX86 7.9.11   Build 001-103-270-812

2022-12-13 12:55:02 18526 28736 INF  12943 NISSLSRV NISSL support started.

 

Set encryption option for j2ee connection url

Open config tool and select from secure store, connection pool, the URL.

Extend URL string with the option: &encryption=true

Save change and close the config tool. Start it again. If you see that the tool was able read the data from DB, this means connection worked.

 

Restart java app.

entry.title: Invoice ERROR
entry.content:
Here is the English translation of the SAP error message text:

Diagnosis

If the following conditions are met, invoice line items received multiple times via EDI/SOA can be settled:

  • Goods receipt–based invoice verification is defined for the purchase order line item.

  • The goods receipt number (delivery note number) is included in the IDoc or SOA message.

  • A differential invoice is used and a previous invoice already exists (as the second invoice). In other words, according to the two-line item concept, for each goods receipt line item or purchase order line item, both the current line item (invoice line item) and the line item from the previous invoice (credit memo line item) are displayed.

System Response

If these conditions are not met, posting of the IDoc or SOA message is canceled.

Procedure

Check the IDoc or SOA message. In some cases, the IDoc or SOA message can be edited (for example, if the supplier accidentally sent the wrong purchase order line item). If it cannot be edited, contact the supplier and request that they resend the invoice with corrected data.

Let me know if you need any adjustments to the translation.

entry.title: Re: Custom Domain Service in SAP BTP Build Work Zone (Standard Edition)
entry.content:
Hi @ChadB 

Very Good Query, below is the answer of your query:

If you try to access the URL "vendor.company.com", it will open the default site that is correct.

To open another site in same work zone instance with custom domain you have to give the site id after the host name of custom domain:

The site id you will get from the site setting as below:

Example: if your site id is "1234" the try to access the below URL:

https://employee.company.com/site?siteId=1234#Shell-home

Please note you have to maintain both the custom domain in the "Saas Routes" configuration of custom domain service. 

I hope this will resolve your issue, Please mark this as solution if it fix your issue also update in the blog.

 

Regards,

Rohit Gera

entry.title: Re: Which Idoc or RFC/ BADI / EXIT triggered to EWM while doing partial usage decision in S4

Hope that was answering your question. Regards Andreass

entry.title: How to Use Multiple APIs in a Single iFlow (SAP Integration Suite)
entry.content:
In real-time integration scenarios, we commonly use multiple APIs within a single integration flow (iFlow). For example, one API response is required as input for another API, or the final response may be the combination of the both API responses.

SAP Integration Suite (CPI) provides components like content modifier, adapters that helps us for multiple APIs scenarios in one iFlow.

In this blog, we will walk through a step-by-step implementation of calling two APIs within a single iFlow, capturing each response, and finally combining or processing the data before sending it back to the final response. 

Overview 

  • user triggers the iFlow using an HTTPS sender 
  • The iFlow calls API 1, captures its response
  • With in response of the API 1 it calls API 2, captures its response 
  • Finally, it processes and returns the combined responses of both APIs 

Step-by-Step Implementation 

Step 1: Create an iFlow 

Create a new Integration Flow (iFlow) in SAP Integration Suite as shown in the below figure. 

HTTPS sender --> iFlow logic (it will sequentially call the two APIs) --> HTTP receiver

 

Step 2: Configure the HTTPS Sender Adapter 

  • In the sender side give the address in the connection tab(endpoint path). It is basically the endpoint used to trigger externally (for example in our case it's postman).
  • In our iFlow I have given the address as /testweatherNote: Make sure the address starts with /<address>

 

Step 3: Configure Content Modifier 1 (API 1 Headers and Properties) 

Message Header 

Use content modifier header to externally call the API using it's host and key values.

Add the host and key value of the first API.

 Exchange Properties 

Use exchange properties to fetch the input values dynamically.

  • Name: Input tag name 
  • Source Type: XPath / Expression 
  • Source Value: XML input tag 

This property will later be used in the API call.

 Step 4: Configure Receiver 1 (HTTP Adapter – API 1) 

Based on the input, response is fetched from the external API via request reply and HTTP receiver adapter.

This request reply fetches the response from the external API and send to the next step of the flow.

  • In the Receiver 1 choose HTTP Adapter.
  • Go to Connections and Provide
    Address:  Address will be the API 1 endpoint 
    Query parameters: In query parameter to fetch the response with dynamic input use ${property.<propertyname>} expression(This allows us to dynamically read the value) .

This step performs the actual call to the first API. 

 Step 5: Configure Content Modifier 2 (Store API 1 Response) 

Add 2nd Content Modifier to store the response of the 1st API.

In the exchange properties add 

  • Name: response1 
  • Source Type: Expression 
  • Source Value: ${in.body} 

This stores the first API response for later use. 

 Step 6: Configure Content Modifier 3 (API 2 Headers)

Now let us call the 2nd API. As done in the earlier step we'll use the content modifier to call the 2nd API

Add the host and key value of the second API.

These headers may differ from API 1 depending on the provider. 

In Exchange property add the properties required to fetch the response from API 2.

Step 7: Configure Receiver 2 (HTTP Adapter – API 2) 

Add request reply and HTTP receiver adapter to fetch the response from the 2nd API.

  • In the Receiver 2 choose HTTP Adapter.
  • Go to Connections and Provide
    Address:  Address will be the API 2 endpoint 
    Query parameters: In query parameter to fetch the response with dynamic input use ${property.<propertyname>} expression(This allows us to dynamically read the value) .

This step triggers the second API call.

Step 8: Configure Content Modifier 4 (Store API 2 Response)  

Add 4th Content Modifier to store the response of the 2nd API.

In the exchange properties add 

  • Name: response2 
  • Source Type: Expression 
  • Source Value: ${in.body}

This stores the response of the second API. 

Step 9: Configure Content Modifier 5 (Final Message Body) 

Add content modifier 5 to store the responses of Both the APIs. This will give us the combined output of both APIs

In Content Modifier 5 Message Body Set Type: Expression and in body add

  • ${property.response1} 
  • ${property.response2} 

This step prepares the final response that combines or processes both API responses.

Step 10: Save and Deploy the iFlow 

  • Save the iFlow 
  • Deploy it successfully 

Step 11: Test Using Postman 

  • Copy the generated HTTPS endpoint 
  • Paste it into Postman 
  • Send the request 
  • Verify the combined output from both APIs  

 

Conclusion/Summary

In this blog we have covered how can we use multiple APIs in a single iFlow with help of content modifier. Also learnt about how to call external API using content modifier headers and reading the dynamic input from the postman using the content modifier exchange properties. We also saw how to take API 1 response as input for API 2 and in final result using content modifier message body we combined the both APIs responses. By this approach developers can design scalable and reusable iFlows in the real time scenarios.

entry.title: Building a Real‑Time Currency Conversion iFlow using SAP Cloud Integration (CPI)
entry.content:
Introduction

While learning SAP Cloud Integration (CPI), I wanted to work on a practical, real‑time use case rather than a basic file‑based or sample integration. In many real projects, applications need to handle multiple currencies because of global customers, suppliers, and financial transactions.

To understand how CPI handles API‑based synchronous integrations, I decided to build a currency conversion iFlow. The objective was to expose an HTTPS endpoint, call an external currency conversion API, and return the converted amount in a readable format. Along with the happy path, I also wanted to implement proper exception handling, similar to what is expected in real production scenarios.

This blog shares my design approach, iFlow structure, and key learnings from implementing this scenario.

Business Requirement

The requirements I considered while designing this iFlow were:

  • Convert an amount from one currency to another in real time
  • Expose the functionality through a secure HTTPS endpoint
  • Fetch up‑to‑date exchange rates from an external API
  • Return the result in a user‑friendly output
  • Handle errors in a centralized and structured way

This closely mimics real‑world requirements where CPI acts as an integration layer between business applications and external services.

High‑Level Solution Overview

I designed the solution using SAP Cloud Integration following a simple API‑to‑API pattern:

  1. An external system sends a currency conversion request.
  2. CPI receives and processes the request.
  3. CPI calls an external currency conversion API.
  4. CPI formats the response as an HTML output.
  5. In case of any failure, CPI handles errors through an Exception Subprocess and sends notifications.

This approach helped me understand how CPI manages synchronous calls, properties, transformations, and error handling together.

iFlow Design Overview

The iFlow is divided into two main parts:

  • Main Integration Flow – handles the successful processing
  • Global Exception Subprocess – handles errors from any step in the flow

This separation made the design clean and easier to maintain.

I-FLow.

Main Integration Flow – Step‑by‑Step

HTTPS_In

I used an HTTPS sender adapter as the entry point of the iFlow. This allows external systems or tools like Postman to send requests in JSON format.

This step starts the message processing and generates the Message Processing Log (MPL) ID, which is useful for monitoring and troubleshooting.

Why I used it:
Most real‑time integrations in CPI are API‑driven, so HTTPS was the natural choice.

HTTPS sender adapter configuration.

You can choose your own address name. 

JSON2XML_Convert

Since the incoming request is in JSON format, I added a JSON to XML converter. This made it easier to work with the payload in CPI, especially for extracting values using content modifiers or XPath.

Learning:
Converting early helps avoid complications in later steps.

Set_ConvParams (Content Modifier)

In this step, I extracted the source currency, target currency, and amount and stored them as Exchange Properties.

I purposely used properties instead of reading values repeatedly from the payload, as this improves readability and reusability across the flow.

Why I did this:
Using properties keeps later steps independent of payload structure.

Content Modifier (Properties tab)

Call_RateAPI

Here, I used a Request‑Reply pattern to call an external currency conversion API. Since the requirement was to return the converted amount immediately, a synchronous call was necessary.

During testing, I intentionally triggered API errors to verify whether my exception handling was working correctly.

Learning:
Request‑Reply is ideal when immediate responses are required.

 

Build_HTML_Result

After receiving the API response, I used a Groovy script to extract the converted amount and build an HTML‑formatted output.

Instead of returning raw JSON or plain text, I formatted the result as an HTML table containing:

  • Source currency
  • Target currency
  • Source amount
  • Converted amount
  • Timestamp (IST)

Why I did this:
HTML output improves readability for users, testers, and demos.

HTTP_Out

Finally, the formatted HTML response is sent back to the caller. This allows the result to be previewed directly in Postman or a browser.

Use case:
This is very helpful during testing and demonstrations without needing a separate UI.

Exception Subprocess – Centralized Error Handling

While building the flow, I realized that handling only the success scenario was not enough. So I implemented a Global Exception Subprocess to handle failures consistently.

Err_Start

This step is triggered automatically whenever an error occurs in any part of the main flow, such as:

  • API failures
  • Groovy errors
  • Payload or conversion issues

Err_Build_HTML

In this step, I extracted error details such as:

  • MPL ID
  • Error type
  • Error message
  • Timestamp

I formatted these details into an HTML table, similar to the success response. This made the error output structured and easy to understand.

Learning:
Readable error messages save a lot of troubleshooting time.

Mail_Out_Err

I added a Mail adapter to send the formatted error details to a support email ID. This ensures that failures are noticed immediately without checking CPI logs manually.

Real‑world relevance:
This is commonly used in production environments for monitoring.

Err_End

The exception flow ends cleanly after sending the notification, ensuring proper message lifecycle completion.

The above one is for incorrect input payload 

Benefits of This Approach

  • Practical understanding of API‑based integrations in SAP CPI
  • Importance of Exchange Properties for clean design
  • How to implement a reusable Exception Subprocess
  • Benefits of HTML‑formatted responses for both success and errors
  • How CPL logs (MPL ID) help in monitoring and support

Conclusion

This exercise helped me gain hands‑on experience in building a real‑time integration scenario using SAP Cloud Integration. By implementing both success and error handling properly, I was able to design an iFlow that closely resembles a real‑world project.

The same design pattern can be reused for many API‑driven integration scenarios, making it a valuable learning experience.

entry.title: Custom rule so that WT /127 and WT /413 brought forward till the last period
entry.content:
Dear Team,

We have processed payments through Infotype 0015 for January 2026 only and currently use two PCRs as outlined below:

* PCR 1 (placed at INN1):

ADDWT *
ADDWT /127

* PCR 2 (placed at INE0):

ADDWTE*

The /127 value is being generated and reflected in Gross Salary.

The payment is not being made in the subsequent months. As a result, neither the /127 value nor the wage type value is consistently updated in Gross Salary.

We would appreciate guidance on creating the correct custom rule in line with SAP Note 3036551 to resolve this issue.

Thank you for your support.

Best regards

entry.title: Re: What should an SD Consultant without experience learn...
entry.content:
Hi @Tatiana_Stingl 
Congratutation. 
I recommend to share your badge and promote your achievements! 
You can share your badge on social media like LinkedIn, Twitter or Facebook. The badge icon can also be added to your email signature. You can also embed it into your own web page or save it locally as a PDF print version. ​

SAP automatically shows your badges in your Learner Profile in SAP Learning Hub and in the One Public Profile on sap.com in case you enable the feature to make your badges visible to others. By allowing this, other users will have a better overview about who you are when they interact with you, e.g. in blogs or discussions on SAP's help portal. ​

Get the maximum transparency by joining the directory(opens in new tab) feature in order to be found by others based on your skills, badges or other search options. 

Kind regards
Margit

entry.title: Re: Invoice ERROR
entry.content:
The error occurs because certain conditions required for processing the invoice via EDI/SOA are not being met. Specifically, the system can only settle invoice line items received multiple times if:

  • The purchase order line item is set up for goods receipt–based invoice verification.
  • The goods receipt number (delivery note number) is included in the incoming IDoc or SOA message.
  • A differential invoice is used, with a previous invoice already existing (so the system can apply the two‑line item concept).

If any of these conditions are missing, the system cancels posting of the IDoc/SOA message.

Next Steps:

  1. Please check the IDoc or SOA message details to confirm whether the goods receipt number and purchase order line item are correct.
  2. If the supplier sent incorrect data (e.g., wrong PO line item), the message may be editable.
  3. If editing is not possible, kindly contact the supplier and request that they resend the invoice with corrected information.
  4. This will ensure the invoice can be processed successfully.

entry.title: Re: Key Figure Editability Horizon in SAP IBP
entry.content:
Hi @renegola92 ,

the editable and non-editable periods for the same Product A are overlapping. This means that the requirements contradict themselves. So if you want to make the periods 12.2025->infinity editable and in the same context 10.2025->infinity (i.e. FY 26), only the union of both works, i.e. 10.2025->infinity. Imho there is no standard way to tell the system for which period level a specific editability horizon shall be applied in case the underlying key figures share the same base planning level.

BR, Peter
